title: Intents
objectives: Understand intents via a real-life analogy
Pass data between activities and get results back
Handle deep links and cross-app actions safely

sections.markdown:
**Think of ordering food.**

- An **explicit intent** is like calling a specific restaurant by name: 'I want to order from Domino's on Main Street.' You know exactly which restaurant. Direct, no ambiguity. In Android: `Intent(this, DetailActivity::class.java)` — go to THIS specific activity.

- An **implicit intent** is like saying 'I want a large veggie pizza' — you don't care which restaurant, whichever one can fulfill it wins. Android sees the request, looks at every app that says 'I handle pizza requests' (declared via **intent filters**), and either picks one or shows a chooser. That's why `Intent(ACTION_VIEW, url)` can open Chrome, Firefox, or the user's default browser — several apps advertised themselves for that action.

- **Extras** are like the toppings and delivery address you attach to the order — `intent.putExtra("userId", 42)`. The receiver reads them with `intent.getIntExtra("userId", -1)`.

- **Result** is like a delivery confirmation coming back — you launched the pizza order and want to know 'delivered' or 'cancelled'. Modern Android uses the **ActivityResult API** to get this back cleanly.

**Why this matters:** intents are how EVERYTHING on Android is triggered — buttons that open new screens, sharing a photo, opening a URL, starting a service, sending a broadcast, taking a picture, picking a contact.

**An Intent** is a messaging object you use to request an action from another component — an Activity, Service, or BroadcastReceiver. Intents can be **explicit** (name a specific target class) or **implicit** (describe an action and let Android find a matching component). Data on intents is carried via **extras** (a Bundle of key/value pairs).
## Explicit intent — you know exactly where you're going
## Implicit intent — 'find someone who can do this'
## Getting a result back — the modern ActivityResult API
## Intent filters — how you advertise your app to Android

sections.code:
<!-- AndroidManifest.xml — DetailActivity handles myapp://user/{id} deep links -->
<activity android:name=".DetailActivity" android:exported="true">
  <intent-filter android:autoVerify="true">
    <action android:name="android.intent.action.VIEW" />
    <category android:name="android.intent.category.DEFAULT" />
    <category android:name="android.intent.category.BROWSABLE" />
    <data
      android:scheme="myapp"
      android:host="user" />
  </intent-filter>
</activity>

<!-- Now `adb shell am start -a android.intent.action.VIEW -d 'myapp://user/42'`
     opens your DetailActivity. Same happens when the user clicks a link in an email. -->

sections.markdown: ## Complete sample program — launcher activity + result-back activity + deep link
## Common beginner mistakes

**Mistake 1: Using deprecated `startActivityForResult` / `onActivityResult`.** Since AndroidX 1.2 (2020), use `registerForActivityResult(...)` — safer, typed contracts, easier to test. New code should never touch `startActivityForResult`.

**Mistake 2: Not checking `resolveActivity` before an implicit intent.** If no app can handle it, `startActivity` throws `ActivityNotFoundException` and crashes your app. Always guard implicit intents.

**Mistake 3: Putting large data in extras.** Extras go through a Binder transaction with a strict size limit (~1 MB total for the intent). Putting a full-quality photo bitmap in an extra will crash. Pass a URI / file path instead.

**Mistake 4: Forgetting `android:exported`.** Since Android 12 (API 31), every component with intent filters MUST explicitly declare `android:exported="true"` or `"false"`. Forgetting this fails to install on newer devices.

**Mistake 5: Using implicit intents for internal navigation.** Sending yourself an implicit intent (`ACTION_VIEW` with your own deep-link URI) opens a chooser if another app also handles it — surprising and risky. Use explicit intents within your app.
## Best practices
**1. Prefer `registerForActivityResult` over `startActivityForResult`.** Modern, typed, testable.
**2. Always guard implicit intents with `resolveActivity` or a try/catch on `ActivityNotFoundException`.** No app is required to have a browser or dialer.
**3. Use `Intent.createChooser` for share actions.** It always shows the picker even if the user has set a default — respectful UX.
**4. Set `android:exported` explicitly on every activity/service/receiver in the manifest.** Required on API 31+.
**5. Use App Links (verified `https` deep links) for production deep linking.** They avoid the 'open with' chooser and go straight to your app.
**6. Pass URIs, not bytes.** Give the receiving app a `content://` URI it can query, not a raw byte blob in an extra.
## Practice programs

**Practice 1 (Easy) — Two-activity navigation.** MainActivity → DetailActivity with an explicit intent, pass a string extra, display it in Detail.

**Why:** The muscle memory for `putExtra` / `getStringExtra`.

**Practice 2 (Easy) — Share text.** A button that opens the system share sheet with `ACTION_SEND` `text/plain`.

**Why:** Standard app feature.

**Practice 3 (Medium) — Result from picker.** Use `PickVisualMedia` contract to pick a photo and display it in an ImageView.

**Why:** Real ActivityResult usage.

**Practice 4 (Hard) — Deep link.** Add an intent filter for `myapp://user/{id}`; trigger via ADB. Extract the id and open the right screen.

**Why:** Every real app has deep-linking.

**Practice 5 (Expert) — App Links.** Set up verified `https://yourapp.com/user/{id}` App Links, host `assetlinks.json`, and confirm the click opens your app without a chooser.

**Why:** Production deep-linking is exactly this workflow.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What is an Intent?**

**A:** An `Intent` is a messaging object used to request an action from another component — an Activity, Service, or BroadcastReceiver. It's the glue that connects Android components across the app and across apps. An intent has an *action* (like `ACTION_VIEW`, `ACTION_SEND`), optional *data* (a URI), optional *categories*, optional *extras* (a Bundle of key/value data), and either a *component name* (explicit) or an implicit filter to match. Intents are how you launch a new screen, share text, open a URL, take a picture, or trigger a background service. Every non-trivial interaction between components uses an intent.

**Q2: Explicit vs implicit intent — difference and when to use each?**

**A:** **Explicit** — names the exact target component: `Intent(this, DetailActivity::class.java)`. Use for navigation WITHIN your app. Safer, guaranteed to work if the target class exists. **Implicit** — describes an action (`ACTION_VIEW` with a URL) and lets Android find a handling component via intent filters. Use for cross-app actions: open URL, share, dial. Multiple apps may match, so the user gets a chooser (or their default handler is used). Rule: explicit for internal, implicit for external. Never implicit for internal — you risk another app hijacking your navigation.

**Q3: What is an intent filter?**

**A:** A declaration in `AndroidManifest.xml` that says 'this Activity/Service/Receiver can handle these kinds of intents.' A filter combines an `<action>`, `<category>`, and optional `<data>` (scheme, host, path). When someone launches an implicit intent, Android checks every installed component's filters and picks matches. Example: adding `ACTION_VIEW + BROWSABLE + scheme=https + host=myapp.com` makes your activity a candidate for opening `https://myapp.com/*` URLs. Filters are how your app becomes discoverable to the rest of the system — sharing, deep-linking, opening files, custom URLs — all rely on them.

**Q4: How do you pass data between activities?**

**A:** Via **extras** — a Bundle attached to the intent. Sender: `intent.putExtra("userId", 42)` (also supports strings, arrays, Parcelables, Serializables). Receiver in `onCreate`: `intent.getIntExtra("userId", -1)`. **Rules:** small data only — extras go through a Binder call, ~1 MB total limit; large blobs will `TransactionTooLargeException`. For large data, pass a URI (`content://`) or an ID and re-fetch from disk/cache. For complex objects, prefer `Parcelable` (faster than `Serializable` on Android) or a data class marked `@Parcelize` (Kotlin plugin generates the boilerplate).

**Q5: How do you get a result back from another activity?**

**A:** Modern (2020+): the **ActivityResult API**. Register a launcher up front in your activity/fragment with `registerForActivityResult(...)`, passing a *contract* (e.g. `ActivityResultContracts.TakePicturePreview()`, `GetContent()`, `PickVisualMedia()`, or `StartActivityForResult()` for custom activities). The registered lambda receives the typed result when the target activity finishes. Launch with `launcher.launch(input)`. Old way (`startActivityForResult` + `onActivityResult` + request codes) is deprecated because it was fragile, untyped, and didn't survive process death cleanly. New apps should never use it.

**Q6: What is a deep link and how do you implement one?**

**A:** A deep link is a URL that opens a specific screen inside your app, either from another app or the browser. Two types: **(1) Custom-scheme deep links** — `myapp://user/42`. Simple, but no verification and can be hijacked. **(2) App Links** — `https://myapp.com/user/42`. Verified via a `.well-known/assetlinks.json` file hosted on your domain, tying the URL to your app's signing certificate. Verified App Links open your app without a chooser. **How to add one:** in `AndroidManifest.xml`, add an intent filter for `ACTION_VIEW + BROWSABLE + DEFAULT` with the URL pattern. In `onCreate`, read `intent.data` to extract the path and route to the right screen. Use App Links (not custom schemes) for anything user-facing in production.

**Q7: What's a `PendingIntent`?**

**A:** A `PendingIntent` is an intent that another app can execute on your behalf, LATER, with YOUR app's permissions and identity. Common examples: (1) a notification's tap action — the system fires the pending intent when tapped; (2) an alarm scheduled via AlarmManager; (3) a widget tap; (4) location or activity update callbacks. Because someone else fires it, security matters — since Android 12 you MUST specify a mutability flag: `FLAG_IMMUTABLE` (recommended default — the receiver can't change extras) or `FLAG_MUTABLE`. Always prefer `FLAG_IMMUTABLE`. Use `PendingIntent.getActivity`, `getService`, `getBroadcast` to create one.

**Q8: What is `android:exported` and why does it matter?**

**A:** `android:exported` in the manifest declares whether other apps can start your component. `"true"` — anyone can launch it via an intent. `"false"` — only your own app can. **Rule (API 31+):** if a component declares an intent filter, `android:exported` MUST be explicitly set or the app won't install. This forces developers to think about which components are reachable from outside, closing a whole class of Intent Redirection vulnerabilities where malicious apps trigger internal-only screens. **Security best practice:** set `exported="true"` only when needed (deep-link entry point, shared content); everything else `"false"`.

## Real-world usage

- **Every share sheet** — `ACTION_SEND` implicit intent.
- **Every deep link** — App Link intent filter → onCreate reads `intent.data`.
- **Notification taps** — a `PendingIntent` that fires the target screen.
- **Widgets** — tap fires a `PendingIntent`; `RemoteViews` provide the layout.
- **Camera capture** — `ActivityResultContracts.TakePicture()` and a Uri to save to.
- **Picking an image** — `ActivityResultContracts.PickVisualMedia()` (modern) or `GetContent("image/*")`.
- **App-to-app workflows** — Uber → Google Maps for directions, News app → Chrome Custom Tabs.

## Advanced notes

- **`Parcelable` vs `Serializable`** — Parcelable is faster and native to Android; `@Parcelize` in Kotlin generates the boilerplate.
- **Custom `ActivityResultContract`** — subclass to build your own typed launcher (e.g. one that returns a domain-typed result).
- **`Intent.FLAG_ACTIVITY_*` flags** — control task/back-stack behavior: `NEW_TASK`, `CLEAR_TOP`, `SINGLE_TOP`, `NO_HISTORY`.
- **Chrome Custom Tabs** — better than `ACTION_VIEW` for showing web content inside your app UX; keeps user's browser state, faster than a WebView.
- **App Links + `autoVerify`** — Android verifies your `assetlinks.json` file at install time; verified links skip the chooser.
- **Deep-link testing** — `adb shell am start -a android.intent.action.VIEW -d 'myapp://user/42'`.
- **Intent Redirection vulnerability** — a serious class of security bugs; validate any Intent you receive from outside your app before starting it internally.

flashcards.back: Explicit names the class (internal navigation); implicit describes an action (cross-app).

flashcards.front: How to pass data between activities?
flashcards.back: `intent.putExtra(key, value)` — small data only. Use URIs / IDs for large data.
Manifest declaration that says 'I can handle these actions/data.'
Custom scheme (`myapp://…`, unverified) or App Links (`https://…`, verified).
Since Android 12, must specify FLAG_IMMUTABLE or FLAG_MUTABLE. Prefer IMMUTABLE.

quiz.q: Which is safer for internal navigation?
quiz.explain: Explicit intents can't be intercepted by other apps.

quiz.q: How to guard an implicit intent?
options: try/catch on ActivityNotFoundException OR intent.resolveActivity != null
quiz.explain: No app is guaranteed to handle it.

quiz.q: Extras have a size limit around…
Verified https deep links are called…

challenges.text: Launch a second activity with an explicit intent and pass a string extra.
Add a button that opens a URL with an implicit intent (guarded).
Use `PickVisualMedia` to pick an image and show it in an ImageView.
Add an intent filter for `myapp://user/{id}` and route to a screen by id.
Return a typed result from a custom activity using `StartActivityForResult` contract.
Set up App Links: intent filter + assetlinks.json on a domain + autoVerify=true.
Build a share-target activity that receives text/plain and image/* — appears in the system share sheet.
